body_txt: In media, whenever a bone that isn't a skull or hip shows up, it's a very good bet it will be the classic femur bone, a straight rod with two evenly-sized bulbs at either end, ignoring the Real Life diversity of ribs, scapulas, clavicles, vertebrae, and all the other diverse bones that hold the body in shape. Possibly justified by the femur being the largest, longest, strongest and straightest bone in the body, so it's instantly recognizable, often well preserved, and useful for everything from improvised clubs to dog chew toys to macabre furniture supports. These simplified femur bones often appear symmetrical, which they are not in Real Life . The top of the femur has one end that connects to the ball-and-socket joint of the hip (called the femoral head ) that is prominent and distinctly shaped. The only portion of the femur which looks like its cartoon rendition is the lower extremity of the bone, with two rounded condyles that form part of the knee joint. In lighthearted works, these are often visible when using X-Ray Sparks , and especially comical if the character's entire skeleton is apparently made of nothing else. Compare Cartoon Meat , which may be on one of these. For the other most over-represented part of the skeleton, see Nothing but Skulls . Not to be confused with an actual animal femur bone used to cook stock.
